\begin{document}

\title{Runtime Safety in Move}
\titlerunning{Runtime Safety in Move}

\author{Victor Gao \and
        Wolfgang Grieskamp \and
        Vineeth Kashyap \and
        George Mitenkov \and \\
        Teng Zhang \and
        Runtian Zhou \and
        Andrea Cappa \and
        Marco Illadi}
\authorrunning{V. Gao et al.}
\institute{Aptos Labs, Palo Alto, USA}

\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Move is a smart-contract language originally developed for the Diem blockchain and now deployed at scale on Aptos.
Move programs execute as fully typed bytecode, which lets the platform statically verify a small set of foundational safety properties --- in particular type safety and reference safety --- ahead of execution, during code loading time.
While this design gives Move strong on-paper guarantees, the static verification logic is intricate, and historically it has been a source of bugs in the platform itself: subtle errors in the verifier can let bytecode through that violates the very rules the verifier is meant to enforce.
In a smart-contract setting, such bugs are not academic: a missed type or reference rule violation can translate directly into loss of assets, forged authority, or unrecoverable corruption of on-chain state.
For this reason, Aptos relies on a second layer of defense in the form of \emph{runtime safety checks} that re-check the critical invariants during execution, providing protection against both compromised bytecode and latent verifier bugs.
This paper describes the runtime safety checks in place for Move on Aptos.
\keywords{Move \and Smart contracts \and Runtime verification \and Type safety \and Reference safety.}
\end{abstract}
